entries.reason: each iteration calls `scale`; loops vectorize only after every call in the body has been inlined away

remarks.headline: NOT vectorized
remarks.reason: each iteration calls `scale`; loops vectorize only after every call in the body has been inlined away
remarks.fix: remove `@noinline` from `scale` (it blocks this loop's vectorization), or hoist the call out of the loop
remarks.verified: simulated removing `@noinline` from `scale` and re-ran the inliner and the optimizer: this loop then vectorizes → vfmadd231ps, 8-wide float32 affine map

remarks.headline: NOT inlined
remarks.fix: remove @noinline if inlining is wanted here

remarks.headline: NOT vectorized
remarks.reason: this is a byte-sum loop, but the vpsadbw kernel accumulates into int64 and this loop's accumulator is narrower
remarks.fix: declare the accumulator as int64 (sum bytes as `total = total + (int64)data[i]`)
remarks.verified: simulated that fix and re-ran the optimizer: this loop then vectorizes → vpsadbw, 32-wide byte sum (AVX2)

remarks.headline: NOT vectorized
remarks.reason: the body contains a nested loop (line 38), and only innermost loops are vectorized; the inner loop did not vectorize either -- see its remark

remarks.headline: NOT vectorized
remarks.reason: this is a float multiply-accumulate (dot-product shape), but no kernel matched its address pattern -- the bases must be plain pointers indexed by the loop counter (base[i])
remarks.fix: hoist invariant index math into a pointer before the loop (e.g. `var row: float32* = &m[r * cols];` then `row[c]`)
remarks.verified: simulated that fix and re-ran the optimizer: this loop then vectorizes → vfmadd231ps, 8-wide float32 FMA dot product

remarks.headline: fully unrolled (8 iterations, constant trip count)
NOT vectorized
remarks.reason: no vectorizer recognized this loop's shape
remarks.fix: vectorizable shapes are unit-stride accesses (a[i], not a[i*k]) over int8/int32/float32/float64 with a straight-line body: maps (a[i] = expr), '+' reductions (s = s + expr), and dot products

remarks.headline: NOT vectorized
remarks.reason: this loop sums int32 values into `s`, but the int32 reduction kernel accumulates into int64 (eight lanes are summed without overflow only there) and `s` is int32
remarks.verified: simulated that fix and re-ran the optimizer: this loop then vectorizes → vpaddd, 8-wide int32 sum (AVX2)

remarks.headline: NOT vectorized
remarks.reason: the loop reads/writes 16-bit integers, and no 16-bit kernels exist
remarks.verified: simulated that fix and re-ran the optimizer: this loop then vectorizes → vpaddd, 8-wide int32 sum (AVX2)

remarks.headline: NOT vectorized
remarks.reason: this is a float multiply-accumulate (dot-product shape), but no kernel matched its address pattern -- the bases must be plain pointers indexed by the loop counter (base[i])
remarks.fix: none via hoisting -- re-checked: the index half that is not the loop counter changes every iteration, so it cannot be hoisted out; this access is genuinely non-unit-stride

remarks.headline: NOT vectorized
remarks.reason: the loop only writes (a fill/init pattern, no array reads); the recognizers cover maps, reductions, and dot products over loaded data, and no constant-fill kernel exists yet

remarks.headline: NOT vectorized
remarks.reason: the loop only writes (a fill/init pattern, no array reads); the recognizers cover maps, reductions, and dot products over loaded data, and no constant-fill kernel exists yet

remarks.headline: NOT inlined
remarks.reason: inlining rounds reached their limit before this call could be revisited

remarks.headline: NOT vectorized
remarks.reason: the loop only writes (a fill/init pattern, no array reads); the recognizers cover maps, reductions, and dot products over loaded data, and no constant-fill kernel exists yet

remarks.headline: NOT vectorized
remarks.reason: the loop reads/writes 16-bit integers, and no 16-bit kernels exist
remarks.fix: use int32 (or int8 if the values fit)

groups.reason: contains the SIMD kernel `simd_affine_map_f32`, which the register allocator doesn't cover yet
groups.consequence: the kernel itself runs at full vector speed; only the scalar code around it keeps values on the stack
groups.fix: nothing for small functions; if a LARGE function mixes a kernel with hot scalar code, move the kernel loop into its own small function so the scalar part keeps the register allocator

groups.reason: contains a call form the register allocator doesn't support yet
groups.consequence: every value in the function is kept on the stack instead of in registers
